title: Storms and Pancakes

jokes.text: Are you two going to get married? / She's way out of my league. / That's the correct answer.
jokes.setup: A crowd member asks Tom and Selina if they're going to get married
jokes.punchline: Tom deflects with 'she's way out of my league,' and Selina immediately affirms: 'That's the correct answer' — revealing her vanity and enjoying the flattery
jokes.explanation: Quick, efficient character beat that establishes the Tom/Selina dynamic immediately. Selina's reflexive self-aggrandizement is perfectly in character. Good economy — three lines, clear laugh.

jokes.text: I mean, within obviously acceptable propriety and so on.
jokes.setup: Tom tells the crowd 'Love you, too' and then immediately hedges
jokes.punchline: The stiff, lawyerly qualification of a spontaneous expression of affection — 'within obviously acceptable propriety and so on' — reveals Tom's controlled, politician-brain nature
jokes.explanation: A deft character joke: Tom can't even enjoy a crowd moment without his political risk-management instinct kicking in. The phrasing is precise and funny — 'obviously acceptable propriety' is bureaucratese applied to love. Very Veep.

jokes.text: If I have any more of those funnel cakes, I won't be able to fit through the bus door. / It's true. Those funnel cakes are just great. / You won't be able to miss us. / It's true because if... we'll eat them and then we'll get so fat.
jokes.setup: Selina makes a self-deprecating funnel cake joke to the crowd which gets a laugh
jokes.punchline: She keeps explaining and elaborating the joke after it already landed, killing it: 'we'll eat them and then we'll get so fat' — the joke dies in real time as she over-explains it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant bit of meta-comedy: Selina makes a joke that works, then destroys it by explaining it. 'We'll eat them and then we'll get so fat' is the sound of a politician without instinct desperately paddling. The structure is perfect — the original joke lands, then the over-explanation is the real joke. Deeply character-specific: Selina cannot read a room or stop talking.

jokes.text: You are the sun, I the moon. Of Jupiter. Not even one of the good ones.
jokes.setup: Tom flatters Selina with a grandiose astronomical metaphor — 'You are the sun, I the moon'
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately deflates it: 'Of Jupiter. Not even one of the good ones.' — she can't even accept flattery without undermining it, and the specificity of 'not even one of the good ones' is hilarious
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Tom delivers a beautiful, sycophantic line. Selina's response is a masterpiece of comedic deflation: she accepts the metaphor but immediately downgrades her VP to the worst possible category. 'Not even one of the good ones' is precisely the right level of specificity — it implies Selina has thought carefully about Jupiter's moon hierarchy. Highly quotable, perfectly economical, deeply character-specific. Comparable to the 8+ anchor.

jokes.text: It's great. Should be called the 'New York Toms.'
jokes.setup: Mike announces a great New York Times profile on Tom James
jokes.punchline: 'Should be called the New York Toms' — a pun that reveals Mike's childish resentment of Tom's media dominance
jokes.explanation: A functional pun that works both as wordplay and character beat — Mike's petty jealousy on display. Not particularly sharp but gets the job done.

jokes.text: You know, a wolf is actually a pack animal. I'm more of a lone squirrel.
jokes.setup: The NYT profile calls Tom 'a lone wolf' — a flattering political cliché
jokes.punchline: Tom corrects the metaphor biologically and then offers the absurd alternative: 'I'm more of a lone squirrel' — a deliberately ridiculous self-deprecation from a man the media is treating as a political god
jokes.explanation: Excellent construction: fact-checks the metaphor (wolves ARE pack animals) and then the self-replacement is deliberately bathetic. 'Lone squirrel' is the right level of absurd — specific enough to be funny, animal enough to extend the metaphor, humble enough to feel like false modesty. Tom's charm offensive rendered comic. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Fun fact... we're running a campaign here, not a bake sale.
jokes.setup: A staffer delivers an irrelevant 'Fun fact' about the Wright Brothers' bicycle shop
jokes.punchline: The cutting response reframes the 'fun facts' as completely useless to the campaign — 'not a bake sale'
jokes.explanation: Functional putdown that establishes the 'fun fact' guy as a running irritant. The bake sale comparison is a decent image but not razor-sharp.

jokes.text: There is sexual tension on this bus. / From? / Tom. / (gasps) Towards? / Me.
jokes.setup: Selina announces there's sexual tension on the bus with conspiratorial gravity
jokes.punchline: Gary's escalating gasps at each revelation — and Selina's total certainty that Tom is into her — sets up the episode's central delusion
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of this exchange is perfect Veep — the staccato Q&A format with Gary's gasps as punctuation. Sets up the Tom/Selina delusion plot efficiently and comically. Gary's 'Right.' after 'Towards? Me.' is a quiet masterpiece of diplomatic uncertainty.

jokes.text: And you saw the way he crossed his legs towards me as soon as I sat down?
jokes.setup: Selina is building her case that Tom has sexual tension towards her
jokes.punchline: The body language 'evidence' is a comically thin reed — Selina has convinced herself that leg-crossing is flirtation, revealing the depth of her self-delusion
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat. Selina reading profound romantic meaning into neutral body language is funny and true to character. Part of the escalating delusion sequence.

jokes.text: We almost... Kissed? / Fucked. / Okay.
jokes.setup: Selina builds dramatic tension teasing a past moment with Tom — Gary assumes the most romantic possibility
jokes.punchline: 'Fucked.' / 'Okay.' — the blunt word choice and Gary's flat, non-judgmental acceptance is perfect
jokes.explanation: The misdirection (we assume 'kissed' is coming) and the flat 'Fucked' lands hard. Gary's 'Okay.' — utterly unfazed — is the perfect button. Maximum economy: four words do everything.

jokes.text: Is it Israel or is it China? / No, it's an actual hurricane.
jokes.setup: Ben says there's a storm brewing — Selina assumes it's a geopolitical crisis
jokes.punchline: 'No, it's an actual hurricane' — the mundane literalism deflates Selina's crisis-mode grandiosity
jokes.explanation: Sharp joke that also reveals Selina's worldview: geopolitical crises are her default mental category. Ben's flat correction is perfectly timed. The joke also works thematically — political storms vs. actual storms is the episode's core irony.

jokes.text: And it's about to take North Carolina from behind.
jokes.setup: Ben describes the hurricane's trajectory toward North Carolina
jokes.punchline: 'Take North Carolina from behind' — crude double entendre delivered deadpan by Ben
jokes.explanation: Standard Veep crude-but-functional gag. Ben's deadpan delivery elevates it slightly. Not particularly original but fits the show's register.

jokes.text: Ma'am, we can track the storm from here. I have a weather app. / We have the full power of the National Weather Service, Mike. / Sure, but this is a really good app.
jokes.setup: The full institutional power of the National Weather Service is invoked
jokes.punchline: Mike's undeterred insistence that his personal app is a legitimate alternative — 'this is a really good app'
jokes.explanation: A running gag seed planted here. Mike's oblivious confidence in his app against the full NWS is excellent character comedy — his incompetence combined with unshakeable self-belief. The payoff comes later.

jokes.text: It's like a morgue full of dead librarians.
jokes.setup: Someone remarks on how quiet the office has become since the campaign staff left
jokes.punchline: 'A morgue full of dead librarians' — escalating redundancy (a morgue already implies death; librarians already implies quiet) creates the comic image
jokes.explanation: Strong simile. The redundancy is the joke — 'morgue' and 'dead' is already tautological, then 'librarians' adds the quiet dimension. The compounding of silence and death metaphors is clever wordsmithing.

jokes.text: Well, great struggling to talk to you.
jokes.setup: Jonah attempts small talk with Sue, who gives nothing
jokes.punchline: 'Great struggling to talk to you' — acknowledging the failure of the conversation as a goodbye
jokes.explanation: Efficient and well-constructed. The inversion of 'great talking to you' is well-earned by Sue's stonewalling. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: This place is leaking bodies faster than a Chinese ferry.
jokes.setup: Jonah laments that all the key staff have left the office
jokes.punchline: 'Leaking bodies faster than a Chinese ferry' — the dark simile comparing staff departures to a maritime disaster
jokes.explanation: A Veep-grade dark simile. The image is vivid and the escalation from office politics to nautical disaster is appropriately absurd. Slightly edgy but in the show's register.

jokes.text: No, don't you dare touch my balls.
jokes.setup: A colleague approaches Jonah saying 'Teddy sends a message' — referring to the man who sexually harassed Jonah by grabbing his testicles
jokes.punchline: Jonah's panicked 'Don't you dare touch my balls' — the PTSD response to the ball-grabbing is both a callback and escalation of the harassment arc
jokes.explanation: Works on multiple levels — crude physical comedy but also genuine discomfort since Jonah was actually assaulted. The show's willingness to play this as both funny and uncomfortable is characteristic.

jokes.text: A joke's not a joke unless everyone can laugh at it.
jokes.setup: Jonah's colleague laughs at the ball-grabbing reference
jokes.punchline: The colleague's defense of the 'joke' — 'a joke's not a joke unless everyone can laugh at it' — is itself ironic since the victim clearly isn't laughing
jokes.explanation: A moment of dark irony — the line is delivered seriously but is obviously wrong (the victim is NOT laughing). Subtle cringe.

jokes.text: This is what I have to look forward to... A bunch of fucking half-wit jokers trying to grab my balls.
jokes.setup: Jonah surveys his new workplace situation after the harassment
jokes.punchline: The existential lament — that this is his career trajectory — delivered with genuine self-pity
jokes.explanation: Jonah's self-pity is his defining comedic register and this fits well. The specificity of 'grab my balls' as the definitive workplace problem is funny.

jokes.text: How are the testies, Jonah?
jokes.setup: Jonah enters an office expecting a professional meeting
jokes.punchline: The congressman greets him by asking about his testicles — the harassment has become his defining public identity
jokes.explanation: Running gag payoff. The casualness with which the question is asked — as if it's normal — is the joke. Efficient cringe.

jokes.text: Put it in that pile over there with everything else. I'll set fire to it later.
jokes.setup: Jonah brings important dairy tariff meeting notes
jokes.punchline: 'I'll set fire to it later' — the casual declaration of planned document destruction as office filing
jokes.explanation: Efficient, dark comedy about bureaucratic nihilism. 'Set fire to it later' as a filing system is a great image. Deadpan delivery implied.

jokes.text: Just look at this guy. He is the new me. He's super me.
jokes.setup: Tom James is shown working a crowd effortlessly while Selina watches
jokes.punchline: 'He's super me' — Selina's grim acknowledgment that Tom is a superior version of herself, delivered with barely concealed horror
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economic line. 'Super me' has the right sting — it's a compliment she desperately doesn't want to give. Selina's self-awareness of her own obsolescence is the episode's dark engine.

jokes.text: I was veep, they polled other candidates, fucked me in my slack old ass. / Sound like a best seller? / I'd read it. / But I'd read anything, though. I love to read.
jokes.setup: Selina bitterly summarizes what her political memoirs would look like
jokes.punchline: Gary's enthusiastic 'I'd read it!' immediately undercut by 'But I'd read anything, though. I love to read.' — the qualification removes all the compliment's value
jokes.explanation: Two-part joke that works beautifully. Selina's raw, crude book synopsis ('fucked me in my slack old ass') is a great image. Then Gary's overcorrection — qualifying his support to the point of meaninglessness — is perfectly in character. The clarification 'I love to read' as if that's reassuring makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I don't want to break a tit. They weren't cheap.
jokes.setup: Concern about falling off a bicycle at the Bike For Health event
jokes.punchline: 'I don't want to break a tit. They weren't cheap.' — the sudden pivot to breast implants as the primary bike safety concern
jokes.explanation: Punchy, character-specific, highly quotable. The specificity of 'They weren't cheap' — treating her body like a financial investment — is very Selina. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: I did do the Tour of Italy 2008, but my mountain times were embarrassing.
jokes.setup: Selina and Tom claim to not bike much, commiserating over their lack of cycling experience
jokes.punchline: Tom casually reveals he completed the Tour of Italy — an elite cycling event — while still framing it as inadequate ('embarrassing mountain times')
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection setup: both claim to not bike much, then Tom casually drops a pro cycling credential while still self-deprecating. Reveals Tom's effortless superiority in all things — the episode's running theme. His obliviousness to how it sounds is the character comedy.

jokes.text: No, I would not say Tom James is a star. I would say he is a superstar. And I made a good pick. Don't you think I did? / Yes, you did. / Good. What do you think? Did I do a good job? / Is this a good choice that I made?
jokes.setup: A reporter asks if Tom James is a star
jokes.punchline: Selina escalates from answering to desperately seeking validation — 'Is this a good choice that I made?' — turning a PR moment into a therapy session
jokes.explanation: Beautifully escalating cringe sequence. What starts as a competent political answer degrades in real time into naked neediness. Each question gets more desperate. 'Is this a good choice that I made?' is the nadir — asked of a reporter with cameras rolling. Perfect Selina character work.

jokes.text: I should be riding in a basket in the front like E.T.
jokes.setup: Selina notices Tom's bicycle is much larger than hers
jokes.punchline: The E.T. reference — the image of the president of the United States being carried in a bicycle basket — is self-deprecating and vivid
jokes.explanation: Good pop culture callback used precisely. The image lands — Selina as an alien being carted around by a superior. Slightly cheap but earns it through specificity.

jokes.text: That's a great image. Vital, dynamic. Self-congratulations are in order.
jokes.setup: Selina is fumbling with her bike while complaining the mirror only shows her eyeball
jokes.punchline: Dry PR-speak applied to a comically unglamorous moment — 'vital, dynamic' — as someone tries to spin Selina's awkwardness as presidential
jokes.explanation: Functional Veep irony — PR language vs. physical reality. 'Self-congratulations are in order' is an odd phrasing that lands with absurdist flavor.

jokes.text: Great. Another hate text from Amy. 'You looked sweaty on TV yesterday. Are you going through the change?'
jokes.setup: Selina's phone chimes during the bike event
jokes.punchline: Amy's text — not policy criticism, not professional grievance, but a personal attack about Selina's appearance and menopause — is viciously petty and perfect
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The specificity of 'Are you going through the change?' as Amy's weapon of choice is exactly right — it's precisely targeted at Selina's vanity, and 'sweaty on TV' is vivid. Amy's character fully expressed in one text. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I wish I understood vendettas. They're so time-consuming.
jokes.setup: Selina receives a hate text from Amy who she fired by sending a press release calling her unstable
jokes.punchline: 'I wish I understood vendettas. They're so time-consuming.' — Selina's complete failure to connect her actions to Amy's reaction, framing the vendetta as Amy's personality quirk rather than justified anger
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and a contender for the series. The complete absence of self-awareness — Selina literally cannot compute why someone she publicly humiliated is angry — is devastating character comedy. 'They're so time-consuming' treats Amy's entirely justified rage as an inefficient hobby. The line is perfectly structured, maximally economical, and could ONLY come from Selina Meyer. Comparable to the 8+ craft anchor.

jokes.text: Hello, I suggested that a week ago and I was shot down like a hang glider over the Pentagon.
jokes.setup: Amy sees news that the campaign is doing a pancake breakfast — the same idea she proposed a week ago and was rejected
jokes.punchline: 'Shot down like a hang glider over the Pentagon' — the specific military-geographic image for being rejected is vividly absurd and Amy's bitterness is perfectly encapsulated
jokes.explanation: Excellent simile. 'Shot down like a hang glider over the Pentagon' is genuinely inventive — the image of a civilian flying machine being destroyed by military force as a metaphor for workplace rejection is both absurd and precise. Amy's DC-coded fury expressed in DC-coded imagery. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Well, I hope that it's a different vibe because I am putting all of that stuff behind me. / Great. We need someone... / Selina Meyer is yesterday's news as far as I'm concerned.
jokes.setup: Amy claims she's moving on from the White House world and putting it behind her
jokes.punchline: She immediately volunteers that 'Selina Meyer is yesterday's news' — proving she cannot stop talking about Selina even while claiming to be done with her
jokes.explanation: Good structural joke — Amy's self-contradiction is immediate and unaware. She says she's over it and then immediately proves she isn't by unprompted bringing up Selina. Classic irony, well-executed.

jokes.text: And a whiter building.
jokes.setup: 'Well, I have more White House staff than the White House'
jokes.punchline: 'And a whiter building' — an actual joke about paint color that also might be a sly racial/political reference, delivered with complete deadpan
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in economy — three words that work on multiple levels. The joke is technically about building color but the subtext around DC's whiteness in every sense is hovering. Delivered perfectly deadpan.

jokes.text: Maybe you can refer to it as the White House since some of our clients actually still like and respect that place.
jokes.setup: Amy calls the White House a 'backstabbing madhouse'
jokes.punchline: Sidney's mild correction reveals the lobbying firm's clientele — people who need access to power and thus can't afford Amy's contempt
jokes.explanation: Functional joke that establishes the lobbying firm's dynamic. Not particularly sharp but earns its keep as character setup.

jokes.text: VP Doyle's a lame duck. And you know what you do with lame animals? / You care for them. / You shoot them dead. / Oh, no, yeah, I got you. Kinder in the long run, actually.
jokes.setup: Dan says Doyle is a lame duck and starts the cliché
jokes.punchline: Amy completes it with 'You care for them' (the humane answer), then Dan counters: 'You shoot them dead.' Amy's cheerful agreement — 'Kinder in the long run, actually' — is the button
jokes.explanation: Excellent three-beat structure. The misdirection (we expect 'you shoot them') is reversed — Amy gives the kind answer, Dan gives the dark one, then Amy's immediate endorsement of the dark answer is character-perfect. The button 'Kinder in the long run, actually' is exactly right.

jokes.text: The night of the green shoes. / Oh, look at you, remembering the shoes. / They gave you blisters. / Or was it... No, it was corns. / Or... / No, it wasn't corns. Not co... I don't get corns.
jokes.setup: Selina raises their near-intimate moment from 12 years ago; Tom remembers the shoes she wore
jokes.punchline: The conversation derails entirely into a clinical foot condition debate — corns vs. blisters — while Selina waits for romantic acknowledgment that never comes
jokes.explanation: A beautifully sustained cringe sequence. Tom's romantic-seeming shoe memory pivots to a medical taxonomy of foot conditions. Selina's vanity — 'I don't get corns' — delivered mid-confession reveals she'd rather correct the foot condition detail than pursue the intimacy. The whole scene subverts rom-com conventions ruthlessly. The corns digression is the funniest part precisely because it's so mundane against the emotional stakes.

jokes.text: The extended cab scene where Selina brings up 'the moment' and Tom remembers only the cab ride, the green shoes, and the corns — not the near-miss.
jokes.setup: Selina has built up 'the moment' with Tom as a near-romantic/sexual encounter 12 years ago; she now raises it directly.
jokes.punchline: Tom's memory of the same night is entirely practical — the cab, the shoes, the blisters/corns — and he walks home because he likes thinking at night. The 'moment' exists only in Selina's head.
jokes.explanation: Extended cringe comedy masterfully constructed. The audience knows the punchline before Selina gets to it — Tom has no idea what she's talking about — making every mundane detail he remembers (corns!) increasingly humiliating. The corn/blisters debate is a perfect comic distraction from the elephant in the room. Selina's face (implied by her subsequent shame) makes this one of the episode's best sequences. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: You wanted to walk home. / Yeah, I love to walk at night. / It's the only time I can think straight, without the phones and screens pestering me.
jokes.setup: Selina has been building to the moment they almost had sex; the climax of the story is 'you decided...'
jokes.punchline: He walked home. That's it. He just walked home. He had absolutely no romantic awareness of the moment. His explanation — he 'loves to walk at night' — is completely genuine and oblivious.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best sustained cringe payoff. The entire 'almost fucked' backstory builds to the devastating revelation: Tom just... walked home. He wasn't even aware there was a moment. His explanation is perfectly innocent and completely annihilating. The long pause before his reply (inferred from timestamp gap [09:51]) amplifies the devastation. This is Veep operating at its peak — genuine emotional humiliation as comedy. Earned completely, no cheap shortcuts.

jokes.text: You okay, ma'am? You look a little flushed. / (gasps) I mentioned the moment in the car. / And? / He had no idea what I was talking about. / No, you're kidding me.
jokes.setup: Gary notices Selina's emotional state upon returning from her private conversation with Tom
jokes.punchline: The revelation — 'he had no idea what I was talking about' — delivered in a pained whisper, with Gary's appalled gasp being the audience surrogate
jokes.explanation: Strong callback payoff. Gary's gasped 'No, you're kidding me' is the perfect audience response — genuine empathy mixed with juicy horror. Sets up the subsequent Gary/Selina exchange.

jokes.text: You want me to talk to him like guy on guy?
jokes.setup: Selina is humiliated and Gary desperately wants to help
jokes.punchline: 'Like guy on guy' — Gary's solution is to leverage their shared maleness, phrased in a way that sounds accidentally pornographic
jokes.explanation: Gary's oblivious double-entendre — he means 'man to man' but says 'guy on guy' — is a small gem. His eagerness to help despite having no capacity to do so is perfect Gary.

jokes.text: I'm going commando at tomorrow's pancake brunch. / What? No underwear? / What? No. Jesus Christ. Yeah, that'll win me the election. A nice shot of my beav.
jokes.setup: Selina says she's going commando at the pancake brunch, meaning no aides
jokes.punchline: Gary takes it literally (no underwear), Selina's appalled correction is itself funny, then she drives the joke home: 'A nice shot of my beav.'
jokes.explanation: Three-beat misdirection joke that escalates perfectly. The misunderstanding is set up economically, the correction is funny, and then Selina's own riff ('nice shot of my beav') shows she can play with the innuendo herself. 'Beav' as presidential vocabulary is inherently funny.

jokes.text: Dan Egan is back. Like denim and measles.
jokes.setup: Dan announces his return from 'exile' to his new lobbying firm colleagues
jokes.punchline: 'Like denim and measles' — both things that never really go away, but the pairing of the mundane (denim) with the dangerous/unpleasant (measles) is the joke
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines of the episode. 'Like denim and measles' is a genuinely surprising and inventive simile — both things that keep coming back, but the contrast between the benign (denim: classic, comfortable) and the pathological (measles: a disease) perfectly captures Dan's nature. He's saying he's both cool AND a contagion. Maximum economy — four words. Highly quotable. Comparable to or exceeding the 8.0 craft anchor.

jokes.text: Lauren, you look beautiful. How do you keep that white wine so cold looking that hot?
jokes.setup: Dan is working the room at his comeback party, laying on the charm
jokes.punchline: The compliment is grammatically contorted — 'cold looking that hot' — and reveals Dan's charm as a mechanical, slightly malfunctioning machine
jokes.explanation: The compliment is trying too hard and the grammatical twist is either brilliant or an accident — either way it works as Dan attempting charm with a slightly broken toolkit.

jokes.text: Well, this is a soulless parade of vanity and ambition. / Isn't it great? Sliders are good, too.
jokes.setup: Amy looks around at Dan's comeback party with disgust
jokes.punchline: Dan's instant, cheerful agreement — 'Isn't it great?' — followed by the bathetic pivot to 'Sliders are good, too' reveals his complete comfort in the soulless world Amy is condemning
jokes.explanation: Excellent two-beat joke. Amy's gothic description ('soulless parade of vanity and ambition') is a great line on its own, but Dan's enthusiastic confirmation — and then the pivot to sliders — perfectly encapsulates his character. He doesn't refute her description; he CELEBRATES it, then moves to food. The word 'too' at the end is the exact right note. Matches the 8.0 craft anchor.

jokes.text: She didn't like my untidiness or my other girlfriends.
jokes.setup: Dan mentions that Nicola Mortlock, who he invited, once moved in with him for a month
jokes.punchline: She left due to 'untidiness or my other girlfriends' — the casual double admission (he's messy AND was seeing multiple women simultaneously) delivered with complete nonchalance
jokes.explanation: Dan's self-revelation as an unreflective cad — he lists 'other girlfriends' as if it's as mundane as being untidy. The equal weighting of tidiness and fidelity is perfectly Dan.

jokes.text: I hope your vagina falls off.
jokes.setup: Nicola greets Amy warmly and is surprised she's not 'in a straightjacket drooling on meds' (as Mike's press release implied)
jokes.punchline: Amy's response to Nicola's friendly greeting: 'I hope your vagina falls off.' — a sudden, extreme, deeply personal curse
jokes.explanation: Stunning in its specificity and economy. Amy's rage — bottled up throughout — erupts at a completely unexpected target (Nicola, who seems friendly) with an anatomically specific curse that is both genuinely shocking and absurdly funny. 'Falls off' as the mechanism of the curse is the right choice — not violent, just... biologically catastrophic. Highly quotable, very Amy.

jokes.text: All right, how do I put this sensitively? Stop acting fucking nuts.
jokes.setup: Dan witnesses Amy's explosive outburst and offers to handle it delicately
jokes.punchline: 'Stop acting fucking nuts' — the promised sensitivity is immediately abandoned; the advice is both the least sensitive possible phrasing and technically correct
jokes.explanation: The setup ('how do I put this sensitively') creates a brief expectation of self-awareness before Dan immediately destroys it. The joke is in the promise vs. the delivery — two beats, maximum efficiency.

jokes.text: Amy screaming in the parking garage / Amy, stop it! / That feels really good. / I don't care.
jokes.setup: Dan advises Amy to 'find a way to release it'
jokes.punchline: Amy releases it — screaming at the top of her lungs in a parking garage — then a cop intervenes; the physical release is both cathartic and mortifying
jokes.explanation: Strong physical comedy. Amy taking the advice completely literally and screaming is unexpected. The cop's intervention and the social embarrassment layer the joke well. 'That feels really good. / I don't care.' is a perfect capper.

jokes.text: Go to a spa, take a Pilates class. Go to a fucking church, find someone there that has some Valium and take four of them.
jokes.setup: Dan is trying to get Amy to calm down after her screaming episode in the parking garage
jokes.punchline: The wellness advice escalates from normal (spa, Pilates) to surreal (go to church specifically to score Valium from a parishioner, take four)
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation. The joke starts with normal self-care and ends with a detailed pharmaceutical acquisition plan involving a church. 'Find someone there that has some Valium and take four of them' is a complete sentence of absurdist logic — the specificity of 'four' seals it. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Thank you. For this and for your broader service.
jokes.setup: A cop has intervened in Amy's screaming episode and told them to stop
jokes.punchline: Dan's formal, diplomatic sign-off — 'For this and for your broader service' — treating the cop's standard noise complaint response as a significant civic contribution
jokes.explanation: The politician's instinct to schmooze even a cop who just told them to shut up — 'for your broader service' is the kind of general appreciation phrase designed to cover any context. Dan can't turn it off.

jokes.text: Jonah Ryan. / This is my personal assistant Richard Splett. / Splett? / Splett. / Uh, Splett.
jokes.setup: Jonah introduces himself and his assistant at what he thinks is a professional meeting
jokes.punchline: The lawyer's double-take on 'Splett' — repeated three times with increasing confusion — as a name that sounds improbable
jokes.explanation: The comedic name-echo works through repetition and the lawyer's bewildered confirmation attempts. 'Splett' is a genuinely funny name — the 'spl' consonant cluster is inherently awkward.

jokes.text: We've all been the victims of Teddy's inappropriate sexual behavior. / Am I the only man that he... / As far as we know.
jokes.setup: The lawyer explains that all the victims of Teddy's sexual harassment are there
jokes.punchline: 'Am I the only man that he...' — Jonah's appalled realization that he's in a room of women, and 'As far as we know' — the lawyer's careful legal non-commitment
jokes.explanation: Sharp joke with a dark edge. Jonah's incomplete question ('Am I the only man that he...') and his trailing off is perfect — he can't bring himself to say it. 'As far as we know' from the lawyer is a great legal hedge that deepens the horror comedically.

jokes.text: There was the ball... The cupping... The grabbing or the... The clutching.
jokes.setup: Jonah tries to characterize what Teddy did as innocent.
jokes.punchline: His attempt to minimize the acts instead catalogs them with increasing specificity — 'ball... cupping... grabbing... clutching' — which is both farcical and darkly serious.
jokes.explanation: The escalating list — each term somehow more specific and damning than the last — is constructed to expose what Jonah is trying to minimize. The ellipses between each word suggest Jonah's pauses as he relives the memory against his will. Dark cringe comedy executing on a difficult subject with precision.

jokes.text: There is no shame here, Jonah. Let it out. / Oh, I understand there's no shame because what happened between Teddy and I was not consensual, obviously. There was the ball... The cupping... The grabbing or the... The clutching.
jokes.setup: The lawyer encourages Jonah to speak freely about Teddy's assault
jokes.punchline: Jonah's clinical inventory of the assault — 'the ball... The cupping... The grabbing or the... The clutching' — delivered with the precision of someone cataloguing damage for insurance
jokes.explanation: The escalating taxonomy of assault — ball, cupping, grabbing, clutching — is grimly funny in its precision. Jonah's hesitations and self-corrections suggest PTSD recall. Dark subject handled with the show's signature commitment.

jokes.text: I understand there's no shame because it was locker room tomfoolery and nothing more.
jokes.setup: The lawyer says there's no shame in what happened to Jonah
jokes.punchline: Jonah immediately reframes his assault as 'locker room tomfoolery' — the self-denial is both funny and character-perfect for someone unable to acknowledge victimhood
jokes.explanation: Jonah's 'locker room tomfoolery' is perfectly chosen — it's the exact phrase a man would use to minimize his own victimization. The show is satirizing male denial of harassment while making it funny through Jonah's specific brand of oblivious machismo.

jokes.text: Jesus fucking Christ. You all look like me. / This is a nightmare. It's a fucking nightmare.
jokes.setup: Jonah enters the waiting room of the lawsuit and sees the assembled victims
jokes.punchline: They are all tall women — and Jonah, a notoriously tall man, sees his female equivalents as a kind of horror mirror. 'You all look like me' is the realization.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual joke. Teddy Sykes specifically targeted tall people — and Jonah, seeing a room of tall women who've been harassed as he was, reacts with existential horror rather than solidarity. 'You all look like me' works on both the physical and psychological level. His calling it 'a nightmare' while these are victims who actually suffered is darkly funny. Strong character integration — Jonah's narcissism prevents him from having empathy even in a moment designed to generate it.

jokes.text: And just so we're clear, at no point was this a job interview?
jokes.setup: Jonah has just fled a harassment lawsuit meeting in a panic
jokes.punchline: His first concern on the way out: whether this could have been a job opportunity he missed — the self-promotional instinct overrides everything else
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Jonah. After fleeing a sexual harassment lawsuit, his immediate concern is whether there was a job on offer. It's a great compression of his character — ambition overrides shame, dignity, or self-awareness. Economy is superb: one question does everything.

jokes.text: Yeah, I feel like I got felt up again, but this time by the law.
jokes.setup: Richard asks how Jonah feels after the lawsuit meeting
jokes.punchline: 'Felt up again, but this time by the law' — extending the sexual assault metaphor to his legal experience, simultaneously minimizing both
jokes.explanation: Good extension of the harassment metaphor. Jonah's frame of reference is now entirely sexual assault — the law becomes another assailant. The casual phrasing 'felt up again' suggests this is becoming normalized for him, which is both funny and dark.

jokes.text: Richard, I don't look like a middle-aged woman, do I?
jokes.setup: Jonah has just seen a room full of tall women who all looked like him
jokes.punchline: The vanity concern that emerges from the experience — not empathy for the victims, but personal aesthetic anxiety about resembling middle-aged women
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jonah coda. Rather than processing what he's seen with any emotional intelligence, his takeaway is purely narcissistic — does he look like those women? Deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: No, sir. / Well, you do have that one purple shirt that looks a bit blousy, but...
jokes.setup: Jonah asks if he looks like a middle-aged woman; Richard says no
jokes.punchline: Richard immediately qualifies — mentioning the blousy purple shirt — making it technically a 'yes, sometimes'
jokes.explanation: Richard's deadpan specificity — he knows which shirt, he knows the exact problem ('blousy') — is perfectly delivered. He's technically reassuring Jonah while undercutting him completely.

jokes.text: He's got that big left ear... He has a small majority and he needs funding for infrastructure. / Okay, really good. It's just it's his right ear that's freakishly large.
jokes.setup: Selina is being briefed on Congressman Cheever as she approaches him — she has his physical and political details almost right
jokes.punchline: 'It's just it's his right ear that's freakishly large' — 'freakishly large' is funny enough, but Selina getting the ear wrong (left vs. right) is the cringe layer
jokes.explanation: Solid walk-and-talk gag. 'Freakishly large' is the word choice that lands. The left/right confusion is a nice cringe detail suggesting Selina might reference the wrong ear to the congressman's face.

jokes.text: Okay, the minute you see bubbles, you flip. / No, you flip when you see holes. / Bubbles is too soon. / Holes, not bubbles.
jokes.setup: The campaign staff is briefing Selina on pancake-making technique for the brunch
jokes.punchline: A deeply earnest debate about the precise moment to flip a pancake — bubbles vs. holes — conducted with the gravity of national security briefings
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the mismatch of tone and content — campaign staff debating pancake physics with the urgency of a policy crisis. 'Bubbles is too soon' delivered as a definitive expert opinion is perfect.

jokes.text: Buttermilk is not a syrup.
jokes.setup: A staffer delivers a 'fun fact' that there are three kinds of syrup: boysenberry, maple, and buttermilk
jokes.punchline: Selina's flat factual correction — buttermilk is not a syrup — delivered as if this is the most absurd thing she's ever heard
jokes.explanation: Four words, maximum economy. Selina is right, which is unusual, and her flat certainty makes it funnier. The 'fun fact' staffer's complete confidence in a wrong fact is the setup; Selina's correction is the payoff.

jokes.text: Who's achin' for some bacon?
jokes.setup: Mike suggests a line for Selina to use at the pancake brunch
jokes.punchline: 'Who's achin' for some bacon?' — the forced rhyme is aggressively cringe; Selina's immediate veto ('No.') is the joke
jokes.explanation: The joke is about Mike's terrible instincts. The rhyme is bad but deliberately bad — that's the point. Has rewatch bonus because Tom delivers the same line later (joke 66) to great crowd effect, making Mike's rejection of his own good idea the irony.

jokes.text: Fun fact... scopolamine was first used as a truth serum by the Czechoslovakian state police. / How is that fun?
jokes.setup: The fun fact about scopolamine is delivered as 'fun'
jokes.punchline: 'How is that fun?' — Selina's flat interrogation of the framing is the joke; information about state police interrogation drug use is not fun
jokes.explanation: The running 'fun fact' gag pays off best here. 'How is that fun?' is the right question — the facts have been getting progressively darker, and Selina finally calls it. Economy perfect.

jokes.text: Did you lose a lot of weight or did you get six inches taller? I think you look great. Great height and weight.
jokes.setup: Tom greets Mel at the pancake brunch with an effusive compliment
jokes.punchline: The compliment is bizarre — weight loss OR growing six inches taller are the only options Tom can see — and then summarized as 'great height and weight' which sounds like a livestock assessment
jokes.explanation: 'Great height and weight' is the line that lands — it sounds like a vet's assessment or a cattle show. Tom's political-charm mode producing slightly inhuman compliments is funny character work.

jokes.text: Well... it's either surprisingly little or surprisingly a lot.
jokes.setup: The fun fact staffer promises to share how much honey a honeybee produces in its lifetime
jokes.punchline: The delivery of the fun fact is: '...it's either surprisingly little or surprisingly a lot.' — the staffer doesn't actually know the fact
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The entire 'fun fact' running gag has been building a character defined by his confident delivery of irrelevant facts — and here he admits he doesn't actually know the fact. 'Either surprisingly little or surprisingly a lot' covers every possible answer while conveying none. The hedged non-answer delivered with the same fun-fact confidence is brilliant. One of the funniest lines in the episode.

jokes.text: Chatting, nodding. This is classic mingling. / Yeah, but it's funeral mingling. Nobody's laughing. She must not be using all the jokes I gave her.
jokes.setup: Selina is working the room at the pancake brunch; the team observes from a distance
jokes.punchline: 'Funeral mingling' — the specific social category of joyless interaction, plus Mike's oblivious confidence that HIS jokes are the solution
jokes.explanation: 'Funeral mingling' is a great coinage. Mike's attribution of the problem to joke underutilization — rather than Selina's inherent limitation — is nicely delusional.

jokes.text: Defocus. / Did I tell you I slept in? / Wow. / Actually, I got up early, but then I just stayed in bed looking at Politico. / That site has turned to shit.
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan are at a spa trying to decompress from DC
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'slept in' is immediately revealed to be a lie — she was up early reading Politico — and Dan's response is to criticize the website rather than address Amy's inability to relax
jokes.explanation: Good character beat — neither Amy nor Dan can actually decompress. Amy's self-correction ('actually I got up early') reveals her attempt at casualness failing. Dan's pivot to Politico criticism is perfectly in register.

jokes.text: Wow, this music is so irritating. / Jesus, only you could be irritated by gamma waves and a whale.
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan are in a spa meant for relaxation
jokes.punchline: Dan's exasperated observation — 'only you could be irritated by gamma waves and a whale' — highlights Amy's incapacity for peace with precise absurdist specificity
jokes.explanation: 'Gamma waves and a whale' is exactly right as a description of spa music — specific enough to be vivid, absurd enough to be funny. 'Only you could be irritated by...' is a great comic construction.

jokes.text: This is just a Swiss suicide clinic with a hint of jojoba.
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan are at a spa, ostensibly to relax
jokes.punchline: 'A Swiss suicide clinic with a hint of jojoba' — the elegant, specific reframe of spa-as-death-preparation, with the beauty product as the distinguishing detail
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best line. 'Swiss suicide clinic with a hint of jojoba' works on every level: the juxtaposition of clinical death and luxury skincare; the precision of 'Swiss' (Dignitas is Swiss — the show knows what it's referencing); 'a hint of jojoba' as the cosmetic garnish on assisted dying. It's maximally economical, deeply original, completely Amy in her gothic nihilism, and functions as a devastating critique of the wellness industry. Would be in any Veep best-of compilation. Comparable to or exceeding the craft 9.5 anchor in construction if not in pure shock value.

jokes.text: Dan, mm-hmm, I did the whole spa thing and it worked. / I have been here, like... / Seven minutes. / Seven minutes. Yeah, it's plenty.
jokes.setup: Amy announces the spa 'worked' and she's ready to go
jokes.punchline: She's been there seven minutes — and her 'Seven minutes. Yeah, it's plenty.' is complete self-delusion delivered with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: Good character beat. Amy's total inability to decompress and her insistence that seven minutes is adequate perfectly captures her DC brain.

jokes.text: Can I get a short stack made without cow's milk? / Are you serious? / No, it makes me gassy. Which, when you have a bag instead of a bowel, is not good.
jokes.setup: A senator requests a special pancake order without cow's milk
jokes.punchline: The medical explanation — 'when you have a bag instead of a bowel, is not good' — reveals a colostomy bag with complete matter-of-fact directness
jokes.explanation: The medical specificity ('a bag instead of a bowel') delivered casually is the joke. Tom's response of just making the pancakes without missing a beat is the button. Dark and specific in the Veep way.

jokes.text: Who's, uh... who's achin' for some bacon?
jokes.setup: Tom delivers the same line Mike suggested for Selina (and she rejected) at the pancake brunch
jokes.punchline: Tom gets a huge crowd response with Mike's exact rejected line — the line Selina dismissed as unusable works perfectly for Tom, deepening the irony of his effortless superiority
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural masterpiece. Mike's line — dismissed by Selina as unusable cringe — becomes a crowd-pleasing hit when Tom delivers it. This retroactively makes Selina's rejection of the line itself the joke: she rejected good advice because it came from Mike and didn't trust her own instincts, while Tom could make anything work. The callback elevates both jokes and crystallizes the Tom vs. Selina dynamic. Exceptional craft — earned completely, character-specific, structural payoff.

jokes.text: No, no, no. That's my holy trinity. Those are my biggies. Without those, all I got is my smallies.
jokes.setup: Sidney has given Amy three of Dan's biggest clients as her reward for landing the new account
jokes.punchline: 'Without those, all I got is my smallies' — 'smallies' as the technical opposite of 'biggies' in Dan's business taxonomy is both infantile and perfectly Dan
jokes.explanation: 'Holy trinity' applied to client accounts is good; 'smallies' as the consequent category is the right level of undignified. Dan's vocabulary degrading under stress is character-specific.

jokes.text: Well, spoils of war, handsome.
jokes.setup: Dan is outraged that his top clients have been given to Amy
jokes.punchline: 'Spoils of war, handsome' — Sidney's utterly unbothered, almost affectionate delivery of this brutal news
jokes.explanation: The word 'handsome' as a form of condescending address after crushing someone is well-chosen. Economy is excellent — four words cover the entire dynamic.

jokes.text: You want to go ahead and give her my parking space and my desk while you're at it? / Now, listen, Dan, I don't want to be unreasonable. Amy, do you want his parking space and desk? / Yes, please.
jokes.setup: Dan sarcastically asks if Sidney will also give Amy his parking space and desk
jokes.punchline: Sidney takes the sarcasm seriously and asks Amy, who immediately says 'Yes, please.' — the rhetorical question becomes literal
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation joke. Dan's sarcasm is weaponized against him when taken literally. Amy's 'Yes, please.' — polite, immediate, completely genuine — is devastating. The structure is airtight: setup (rhetorical), beat (Sidney's pivot), punchline (Amy). Matches the 8.0 craft anchor.

jokes.text: You are Beyoncé. He is backup bootie.
jokes.setup: Selina is demoralized watching Tom dominate the pancake brunch
jokes.punchline: 'He is backup bootie' — Gary's pep talk compares Tom to a background dancer's posterior, delivered with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: 'Backup bootie' as the specific diminishment chosen for Tom James is perfect Gary — the comparison is meant to be uplifting but accidentally suggests Tom is merely background anatomy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Ma'am, you've got to get back to DC faster than a speeding drunk.
jokes.setup: Ben calls to urgently recall Selina to DC for the hurricane
jokes.punchline: 'Faster than a speeding drunk' — the comparison class for urgency is an impaired driver; the image is vivid and specifically Ben
jokes.explanation: Ben's similes are a character signature, and this one delivers. 'Speeding drunk' is exactly the right combination of fast and dangerous, with Ben's world-weary cynicism embedded in the comparison.

jokes.text: Yeah, six hours till flying cow time.
jokes.setup: Ben is describing the severity of the incoming hurricane
jokes.punchline: 'Flying cow time' — a technical-sounding but utterly absurd metric for hurricane severity
jokes.explanation: 'Flying cow time' is a great image — simultaneously specific (cows are heavy; if they're flying, the wind is severe) and absurd. Delivered by Ben as a real measurement makes it funnier.

jokes.text: This is a potential tragedy that could be great for you.
jokes.setup: Ben is briefing Selina on the hurricane and its political implications
jokes.punchline: 'A potential tragedy that could be great for you' — the political calculation laid bare with zero pretense
jokes.explanation: Veep at its satirical best. The sentence holds two contradictory registers — human tragedy and political opportunity — in perfect balance, with no ironic distance, no shame. Ben saying the quiet part loud. Highly quotable, genuinely sharp political satire.

jokes.text: Hell, yes. I mean, no offense meant, Sue, but when it's just you and me, it's like it's just me.
jokes.setup: Ben says he's excited the campaign is coming back to DC because of the hurricane
jokes.punchline: 'When it's just you and me, it's like it's just me' — the devastating assessment of Sue's conversational presence (or lack thereof)
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed. The logical form ('it's like it's just me') is airtight — two people, one of whom doesn't register as a presence = one person. The callback to the opening morgue/librarian quiet establishes Sue as the episode's running stone-wall gag, and Ben's frank assessment is the best payoff. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Both the National Weather Service and my app are saying this.
jokes.setup: Mike has been defending his weather app throughout the episode
jokes.punchline: Mike now presents the app as equivalent to the National Weather Service — his final vindication — but it's still funny that this is his frame
jokes.explanation: Running gag payoff. Mike has waited his moment — the app is finally right. The comedy is in how much he values the app's agreement with the NWS as if they're equals.

jokes.text: Either they have the contrast turned up to max or you are looking fine.
jokes.setup: Tom greets the Governor of North Carolina, Cecile, on a video call
jokes.punchline: The compliment with a technical hedge — 'contrast turned up to max' — is Tom's smooth charm rendered ever-so-slightly off by the video call caveat
jokes.explanation: Tom's charm operating perfectly — he hedges the compliment enough to sound genuine while still landing the flattery. The technical screen-quality caveat is exactly right. Very Tom.

jokes.text: I have had it with her category five fucking snoot. / And... she definitely can't hear us? / No. / Maybe she can lip-read.
jokes.setup: Selina vents about the Governor while on a video call with her
jokes.punchline: 'Maybe she can lip-read' — the belated paranoia about being caught, combined with 'category five fucking snoot' as a hurricane-themed insult
jokes.explanation: 'Category five fucking snoot' is a great coinage — using the hurricane scale to rate someone's snootiness is inventive and topical. 'Maybe she can lip-read' arriving after they've been venting extensively is perfect paranoid-cringe timing.

jokes.text: Okay, um... let's just swivel the chairs. / [beat] / Jesus Christ. / She know that we can hear that? / My guess would be no. / I got a text from Ben. / What did he say? / He says he can't hear anything.
jokes.setup: The team tries to have a private conversation while on video call with the Governor; Tom suggests swiveling chairs
jokes.punchline: The swiveling chairs don't work as a privacy solution — everyone can still hear everything, including Selina's venting about the Governor — and Ben's text confirms the audio is fine while they operate under the illusion of privacy
jokes.explanation: Good escalating cringe sequence. The chair swivel as a privacy solution is absurd. The layered communication — texting Ben to find out if the Governor can hear them — while the Governor presumably can — is good structural comedy.

jokes.text: Just throwing that into the barbecue sauce.
jokes.setup: Tom suggests that grounding flights would disadvantage Senator O'Brien who is in Florida, framing it as a casual political observation
jokes.punchline: 'Just throwing that into the barbecue sauce' — an apparently Southern/folksy idiom that doesn't quite mean anything, delivered with complete smoothness by Tom
jokes.explanation: Excellent invented idiom. 'Into the barbecue sauce' doesn't exist as a phrase — it sounds like it should mean something folksy but doesn't. Tom delivers naked political calculation wrapped in faux-casual idiom, and the phrase is so confident it almost passes. Highly quotable precisely because it's nonsense delivered as wisdom.

jokes.text: It doesn't work. The chair...
jokes.setup: The team has swiveled their chairs to discuss things privately.
jokes.punchline: Selina realizes mid-conversation that swiveling chairs doesn't solve anything — it just makes everyone face away from each other on camera.
jokes.explanation: Callback completion — the obvious non-solution fails as predicted. The humor is in Selina's delay in recognizing this. The trailing ellipsis suggests the physical comedy of her realizing mid-swivel.

jokes.text: My app says moderate breeze. / These trees are still standing. / I don't want to look at standing trees.
jokes.setup: The team arrives in North Carolina to find almost no storm damage
jokes.punchline: 'I don't want to look at standing trees' — Selina's statement as a weather complaint and political disaster simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Brilliant compression. 'I don't want to look at standing trees' is a perfect political satire line — a president disappointed by the absence of disaster because disaster = political opportunity. The app callback also pays off: Mike's app was vindicated but in the worst possible way. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: How about that app of yours, Mike? You better get your money back on that one. / It was actually free. / I updated my software and it just appeared on my screen. And I was like, 'What is this thing?' And I just picked it up.
jokes.setup: Selina blames Mike's app for the failed storm-chasing mission
jokes.punchline: Mike's defense: the app was free; he didn't even choose it — it just appeared after a software update. The explanation makes his advocacy for it as a legitimate forecasting tool even more absurd in retrospect.
jokes.explanation: Perfect running gag payoff. Mike spent the entire episode defending an app that he didn't choose, didn't pay for, and barely understood. His oblivious explanation of how it appeared — 'I was like, What is this thing? And I just picked it up' — is both funny and damning.

jokes.text: No, it certainly didn't. / It fell at the first fence and it got shot. And now some French fucker's got it in a baguette.
jokes.setup: Tom tries to soften the failed storm-chasing gamble with a horse racing metaphor: 'sometimes you have to gamble / unfortunately, our horse did not win'
jokes.punchline: Selina extends the metaphor with vicious precision: it didn't just lose, it 'fell at the first fence and got shot. And now some French fucker's got it in a baguette.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top two or three jokes. Selina takes Tom's equine metaphor and escalates it through three perfect beats: the horse falls, is shot (humane destruction), and then ends up as horse meat in a French baguette. Each beat is more specific and devastating than the last. 'Some French fucker's got it in a baguette' lands with maximum impact — 'French fucker' is the right insult, 'baguette' is the right vehicle. Highly quotable, beautifully structured escalation, could only be Selina.

jokes.text: Is this a joke? / Honestly, why are you here? / How tall are you?
jokes.setup: Jonah has a meeting with Louise Benjamin from Cabinet Affairs in North Carolina.
jokes.punchline: Louise's three-beat reaction to seeing Jonah — 'Is this a joke? / Honestly, why are you here? / How tall are you?' — is a cascade of incredulity from professional to existential.
jokes.explanation: The escalating incredulity — from professional ('Is this a joke?') to philosophical ('Honestly, why are you here?') to physical ('How tall are you?') — is a perfect Jonah Ryan introduction to a new character. The final question pivots from existential to physical as if his height is the strangest thing about him, which it both is and isn't.

jokes.text: Hello. Is this a joke? / I'm sorry? / Honestly, why are you here? / I'm collating agency responses... / How tall are you?
jokes.setup: Jonah is getting a campaign visit briefing from Louise Benjamin of Cabinet Affairs
jokes.punchline: Jonah immediately fixates on her height — 'How tall are you?' — because she's tall and he's just come from a room full of tall women who were Teddy's harassment victims
jokes.explanation: Good callback. Jonah's PTSD about tall women — and his completely inappropriate response to Louise — is character-consistent and structurally earned by the earlier scene.

jokes.text: I am sorry, ma'am. A number of tall women were molested and Mr. Ryan was one of them.
jokes.setup: Louise is baffled by Jonah's fixation on her height
jokes.punchline: Richard's sincere, helpful explanation — delivered deadpan — manages to simultaneously (a) be factually accurate, (b) incorrectly classify Jonah as a woman, and (c) be utterly mortifying
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. Richard's explanation perfectly captures his character — helpful, literal, completely oblivious to social norms. The inadvertent classification of Jonah as one of the 'tall women' is the devastating punchline. 'Mr. Ryan was one of them' said of a room of women is perfect deadpan. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Ma'am, we've also set up a tour of an egg plant. I mean a plant that packs eggs, not the big vegetable. / Thanks for clarifying.
jokes.setup: An aide announces a tour of an eggplant
jokes.punchline: The correction — 'not the big vegetable' — and Selina's dry 'Thanks for clarifying' at the expense of the aide's unneeded explanation
jokes.explanation: Serviceable wordplay. 'Not the big vegetable' as an aide's clarification is mildly funny. 'Thanks for clarifying' is the right dry response but doesn't elevate it much.

jokes.text: Nope, I'm gonna be hiding in the back of a car. I know. Biography just writes itself.
jokes.setup: Selina is pivoting from presidential damage assessment to political campaigning
jokes.punchline: 'Biography just writes itself' — the aide's deadpan acknowledgment that Selina hiding in a car during a disaster is not exactly heroic memoir material
jokes.explanation: Good dry observation. The biography joke works against the earlier memoir pitch from Selina. The sarcasm is light but well-placed.

jokes.text: (quietly) This isn't damage. Just some shitty building.
jokes.setup: Selina is walking through the 'storm damaged' egg plant for cameras
jokes.punchline: Her whispered assessment — 'this isn't damage, just some shitty building' — reveals the damage is the building's pre-existing state
jokes.explanation: Sharp comic observation. The truth — this is just a poor building — whispered while performing concern for cameras is classic Veep hypocrisy played perfectly.

jokes.text: O'Brien just pulled a teddy bear out of the ruins of a house.
jokes.setup: Selina is making the best of minimal storm damage; rival O'Brien is in Florida with the actual hurricane
jokes.punchline: The devastating optics comparison — O'Brien has a teddy bear from actual ruins vs. Selina's 'shitty building' — delivered as a flat information statement
jokes.explanation: The political optics joke lands perfectly because of specificity — a teddy bear from ruins is the exactly the image that wins elections. The flat delivery of catastrophic news makes it funnier. Selina's situation perfectly crystallized in one line.

jokes.text: Complete 180. This is a campaign visit. Go get Tom now.
jokes.setup: Moments ago Selina called this a presidential visit to avoid looking like it's campaigning. Then the O'Brien teddy bear moment happens.
jokes.punchline: Selina instantly reverses: 'complete 180, this is a campaign visit, go get Tom now' — political calculation exposed in real time.
jokes.explanation: The rapid reversal of stated position — purely driven by the competitor's actions — is sharp political satire. 'Complete 180' said out loud to your staff is funny in its shamelessness.

jokes.text: Tom chatting to worker Patrick: 'I've been looking for you.' Then deep conversation about embossing dentil moulding while Selina stands ignored.
jokes.setup: Tom and Selina are doing a campaign visit; Selina is supposed to be the star
jokes.punchline: Tom gets completely absorbed in a genuine conversation about woodworking while Selina is left standing — the absurdity of the VP being more engaged with dentil moulding than the presidential candidate
jokes.explanation: The comedy of Tom being genuinely interested in the worker's craft — while Selina is politically performing interest — is well-constructed irony. Tom keeps upstaging Selina even when doing something completely mundane.

jokes.text: Are you able to emboss harder species of wood? / Yeah, we emboss hard maple right here. / Can you combine that? / The emboss and the dentil moulding? / Yes, sir, you can.
jokes.setup: Tom is supposed to be doing a quick campaign appearance at a moulding factory
jokes.punchline: Tom has become genuinely fascinated by embossed dentil moulding — he's asking expert-level follow-up questions while the political operation waits — his authenticity is his own worst enemy
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy. Tom's genuine interest in the manufacturing process — taking time to learn about hard maple embossing — is played as both a virtue and a liability. The contrast with political calculation elsewhere in the episode is the joke.

jokes.text: Sue around? / [long pause / reaction beat]
jokes.setup: Someone arrives at the White House office and asks if Sue is around
jokes.punchline: The extended pause — Sue is presumably present but responding with her characteristic non-acknowledgment — is the joke; the asker's uncertainty about whether anyone is there
jokes.explanation: The Sue-is-a-void gag running through the episode gets a brief payoff here. The joke is entirely in the silence — Sue's non-response to her own name being called is consistent with her established character.

jokes.text: It's too ornate, right? / Exactly. / You don't want to do it. I know I don't. / Hey, I'm gonna have to cut you off. / He's a chatterbox, isn't he? / Yes, he is.
jokes.setup: Tom has been deep in conversation with the factory worker; Selina and Gary observe from behind
jokes.punchline: Tom calls the worker a 'chatterbox' — when Tom has been the one asking questions and driving the conversation; Selina's strained 'Yes, he is' confirms the irony
jokes.explanation: Good character irony. Tom attributes the over-chatting to the worker when he's the one who kept asking follow-up questions. Selina's agreement is tight-lipped diplomacy. A nice closing joke that wraps the Tom-as-oblivious-force-of-nature theme.

jokes.text: You don't have to say hi to them. We've already said hi to these folks. Come on.
jokes.setup: Tom is still pressing the flesh with factory workers as the campaign tries to wrap up
jokes.punchline: Selina's barely-concealed exasperation as she herds Tom — 'we've already said hi to these folks' — while trying to look enthusiastic for cameras
jokes.explanation: A functional closing cringe beat. Selina corralling Tom like an enthusiastic child while trying to maintain presidential dignity is the episode's final image of their dynamic.

tmdb_overview: Selina is annoyed by the press' reaction to her running mate's charm.
insight: Cringe-heavy 58-joke episode leans harder on discomfort than Veep's typical satire strength.
